narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was carefully evaluated by our admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on key academic and personal factors from past students. Your profile includes strong full-time enrollment plans, an undergraduate GPA of 2.90, an LSAT score of 29, and notably low rankings in your school's deciles for Year 1 and Year 3 grades—placing you in the bottom decile for both, far below the typical student's mid-range performance. The model estimates a 79.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is why your application was rejected; we prioritize admitting those predicted to pass and succeed in their legal careers. The biggest factors pushing toward this failure prediction were your Year 3 decile ranking, which had the strongest negative influence by signaling weaker performance relative to peers, followed closely by your Year 1 decile, which similarly highlighted early academic struggles compared to the average student. In contrast, your family income level nudged the prediction slightly toward success, as upper-middle-class backgrounds often correlate with better outcomes in the dataset. Your undergraduate GPA and LSAT score also provided modest positive pulls toward passing, though not enough to offset the early grade concerns, especially since your LSAT is well below the dataset average of 36.7 and your GPA trails the typical 3.21. Full-time status offered a tiny boost, aligning with the vast majority of successful students who pursue it. Overall, your situation stands out because top influencers like those low deciles deviate sharply from the stronger academic trajectories of admitted applicants, tipping the scales despite some favorable elements. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar starts through targeted improvement. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your academic record with additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could significantly shift your prediction in future cycles. We're rooting for your success in law.
